does: This paper introduces AgentFloor, a 30-task benchmark organized as a six-tier capability ladder to systematically evaluate which agentic tasks require large frontier models versus smaller ones. The benchmark spans instruction following, tool use, multi-step coordination, and long-horizon planning under persistent constraints. The authors evaluate 16 open-weight models ranging from 0.27B to 32B parameters alongside GPT-5, conducting 16,542 scored runs. The core finding is that small and mid-sized open-weight models sufficiently handle the short-horizon, structured tool-use work that dominates real production agent pipelines, while the strongest open-weight model matches GPT-5 performance on the benchmark at substantially lower computational cost. Performance gaps emerge primarily on long-horizon planning tasks requiring sustained constraint satisfaction.
connects: This work responds to the practical limitation of existing agent evaluation frameworks, which do not reflect the actual distribution of model calls in production systems where most calls are short and routine. It builds on prior research in agent benchmarking and tool-use evaluation while extending these to explicitly address the routing question of model efficiency. The paper engages with broader lines of work on open-weight model capabilities, instruction following, and multi-step reasoning that have expanded our understanding of what smaller models can achieve. It also connects to research on agentic systems and their computational efficiency, contributing empirical evidence to debates about frontier model necessity versus cost-benefit tradeoffs in deployed systems.
enables: This work establishes a methodology and benchmark for systematically characterizing model capability requirements across agent workflow stages, enabling more efficient production system design. The capability ladder framework could support future research into optimal model routing strategies, allowing systems to allocate frontier models only where genuinely necessary. The benchmark itself facilitates continued evaluation as smaller models improve, tracking the capability frontier at different scales. The results open questions about what architectural or training differences enable long-horizon planning, pointing toward targeted improvements for smaller models. This work also enables cost-benefit analysis for practitioners designing agentic systems, potentially shifting industry practice toward more heterogeneous model deployments that maximize efficiency without sacrificing quality.
